content:
# Exodus Chapter 11 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~37r–37v (PDF pp. 72–73); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 51–52 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_011_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_011_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_011_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_011_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Announcement of the Tenth Plague (vv. 1–10)

**1** And the LORD said to Moses, "One more plague [**nega echad**] I will bring upon Pharaoh and upon Egypt. Afterward he will send you out from here. When he sends you out completely, he will surely drive you out from here.

**2** Speak now in the ears of the people, and let them ask [**sha'al**], each man from his neighbor and each woman from her neighbor, articles of silver and articles of gold."

**3** And the LORD gave the people favor in the eyes of Egypt. Also the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the eyes of the servants of Pharaoh and in the eyes of the people.

**4** And Moses said, "Thus says the LORD: 'About midnight I am going out into the midst of Egypt,

**5** and every firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits upon his throne to the firstborn of the maidservant who is behind the millstones [**achar harechayim**], and every firstborn of the beast.

**6** And there shall be a great cry in all the land of Egypt, such as has never been and such as shall never be again.

**7** But against all the sons of Israel not a dog shall sharpen [**yecheratz**] its tongue, against neither man nor beast — so that you may know that the LORD makes a distinction [**yafhleh**] between Egypt and between Israel.'

**8** And all these servants of yours shall come down to me and bow down to me, saying, 'Go out, you and all the people who are at your feet.' And afterward I will go out." And he went out from Pharaoh in hot anger [**bechari-af**].

**9** And the LORD said to Moses, "Pharaoh will not listen to you, so that My wonders may be multiplied [**revot**] in the land of Egypt."

**10** **And Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh. And the LORD strengthened [chazaq, Piel] the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not send out the sons of Israel from his land.**

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~37r (PDF p. 72) contains the concluding verses of Exodus 10 in its upper columns and transitions into Exodus 11 in the lower portion of the page. The three-column layout continues with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. A setumah (closed paragraph) marker is visible at the transition between the end of Exodus 10:29 and the opening of 11:1, indicating continuity within the broader narrative unit — the conversation between Moses and Pharaoh in 10:28-29 flows directly into the divine speech of 11:1, a feature that has long been noted by commentators (some argue that 11:1-3 is a flashback or parenthetical insertion within the final confrontation of 10:24-29). The second page (folio ~37v, PDF p. 73) carries the remainder of chapter 11 and the beginning of chapter 12. A petuchah (open paragraph) marker appears before 12:1, indicating the major thematic break between the plague announcement and the Passover instructions. Masorah parva notes appear in the margins, including annotations on the rare form yecheratz (v. 7) and on the phrase nega echad (v. 1). The Codex Vaticanus pages 51-52 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 11. The text on page 51 continues from the end of chapter 10 into the opening of chapter 11, with the chapter boundary visible. Page 52 carries the remainder of chapter 11 and transitions into the Passover legislation of chapter 12. The LXX text of Exodus 11 shows notable divergences from the MT, particularly in the arrangement and placement of verses — the LXX includes material corresponding to 11:1-3 at a different point in the narrative sequence (partially within chapter 10), reflecting an alternative tradition of text arrangement. The Vaticanus text also shows expansions in v. 3 regarding Moses' standing in Egypt.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"One more plague" (v. 1) — nega echad:** Hebrew vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh od nega echad avi al-Par'oh ve'al-Mitsrayim acharey-khen yeshalach etkhem mizzeh. The noun nega ("plague, stroke, blow") differs from the more common makkah ("blow, plague") used elsewhere in the plague narrative. The term nega carries the connotation of a divine touch or stroke — it is the same word used for skin afflictions in Leviticus 13-14 and for divine judgment-touches on houses (Lev. 14:34). The modifier echad ("one") is emphatic: precisely one more, and no more. The phrase acharey-khen yeshalach etkhem mizzeh ("afterward he will send you out from here") is immediately followed by the escalation: keshallecho kalah garesh yegaresh etkhem mizzeh ("when he sends you out completely, he will surely drive you out from here"). The Piel infinitive absolute garesh with the Piel imperfect yegaresh creates an emphatic construction — Pharaoh will not merely permit departure but will forcefully expel Israel. The shift from "send out" (shalach) to "drive out" (garash) signals the reversal of the entire plague-narrative dynamic: the one who refused to send out will end by driving out. LOGOS renders "one more plague" for nega echad and "drive you out" for garesh yegaresh to preserve this escalation. The LXX renders eti mian plēgēn epaxō epi Pharaō ("yet one more plague I will bring upon Pharaoh"), using plēgē ("blow, plague") for nega.

2. **"Let them ask" (v. 2) — sha'al, not "borrow":** Hebrew dabber-na be'ozney ha'am veyish'alu ish me'et re'ehu ve'ishah me'et re'utah keley-khesef ukheley zahav. The verb sha'al means "to ask, to request" — not "to borrow" (as in the KJV and many traditional renderings). The distinction is theologically significant: "borrowing" implies deception (they never intended to return the items), while "asking" implies a straightforward request. The Israelites are instructed to ask their Egyptian neighbors for articles of silver and gold. The verb carries no obligation of return — this is a request, not a loan. The context of v. 3 (God gave the people favor) makes clear that the Egyptians will give willingly, moved by divine influence. This "asking" also fulfills the promise of Genesis 15:14: "they shall come out with great possessions." The specification of ish me'et re'ehu ve'ishah me'et re'utah ("each man from his neighbor and each woman from her neighbor") indicates personal, face-to-face requests — and reveals that Israelite and Egyptian families lived in proximity, not in strict segregation. LOGOS renders "ask" to preserve the straightforward meaning of sha'al. The LXX renders aitēsatō ("let each one ask/request"), using aiteō, which also means "to ask, to request" — confirming the non-deceptive meaning.

3. **"The LORD gave the people favor in the eyes of Egypt" (v. 3) — chen be'einey Mitsrayim:** Hebrew vayyitten YHWH et-chen ha'am be'einey Mitsrayim. The noun chen ("favor, grace, charm") is given by God — this is divinely bestowed attractiveness. The Egyptians do not naturally feel well-disposed toward the people who have been the occasion of ten devastating plagues; rather, God supernaturally instills this favorable disposition. The phrase be'einey Mitsrayim ("in the eyes of Egypt") uses the collective national name — all Egypt, as a single entity, looks upon Israel with favor. The second clause — gam ha'ish Mosheh gadol me'od be'erets Mitsrayim be'einey avdey-Far'oh uve'einey ha'am ("also the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the eyes of Pharaoh's servants and in the eyes of the people") — is a remarkable editorial aside. The narrator pauses to comment on Moses' reputation. The phrase ha'ish Mosheh ("the man Moses") is both reverential and humanizing: Moses the man, as a human individual, has achieved extraordinary stature. That he is great "in the eyes of the servants of Pharaoh" is especially significant — these are the same servants who challenged Pharaoh in 10:7, and their esteem for Moses contrasts sharply with Pharaoh's hostility. LOGOS renders literally. The LXX renders kai edōken kurios tēn charin tou laou autou enantion tōn Aiguptiōn ("and the Lord gave the favor of His people before the Egyptians"), adding autou ("His") — making explicit that these are God's people.

4. **"About midnight" (v. 4) — kachatsot hallaylah:** Hebrew koh amar YHWH kachatsot hallaylah ani yotse betokh Mitsrayim. The phrase kachatsot hallaylah ("about the dividing of the night," i.e., about midnight) uses chatsot from the root ch-ts-h ("to divide, to halve"). Midnight is the dividing-point of the night — the moment when darkness reaches its deepest extent before beginning to recede. That God acts "about midnight" (the preposition ke- indicates approximation) connects to the darkness plague just concluded: the God who sent supernatural darkness now acts at the darkest natural hour. The declaration ani yotse betokh Mitsrayim ("I am going out into the midst of Egypt") uses the active participle yotse — God is personally going out. This is not an angelic delegation (though cf. 12:23 where the mashchit, "the destroyer," appears) but a divine sortie. The phrase betokh Mitsrayim ("into the midst of") emphasizes penetration — God enters the heart of Egypt. LOGOS renders "about midnight" to preserve the approximation. The LXX renders peri mesas nuktas ("about the middle of the night"), closely matching the MT.

5. **"From the firstborn of Pharaoh... to the firstborn of the maidservant who is behind the millstones" (v. 5) — the social spectrum:** Hebrew umet kol-bekhor be'erets Mitsrayim mibbkhor Par'oh hayyoshev al-kis'o ad bekhor hashifchah asher achar harechayim vekhol bekhor behemah. The plague strikes across the entire social hierarchy: from Pharaoh who "sits upon his throne" (hayyoshev al-kis'o) — the pinnacle of Egyptian power — to the maidservant "who is behind the millstones" (asher achar harechayim) — the lowest social position, a female slave performing the most menial labor. The rechayim ("millstones," dual form) refers to the hand-mill, a pair of grinding stones operated by the lowest-ranking household servants (cf. Isa. 47:2; Matt. 24:41). The phrase achar harechayim ("behind the millstones") places the maidservant physically behind the grinding apparatus — bent over the stones in her labor. Every social stratum between the throne and the millstones is encompassed. The addition of vekhol bekhor behemah ("and every firstborn of the beast") extends the plague beyond the human realm — the death touches even the animal kingdom, reprising the livestock plague (9:1-7) but now targeting specifically the firstborn. LOGOS renders "the firstborn of the maidservant who is behind the millstones" to preserve the full Hebrew construction. The LXX renders heōs prōtotokou tēs therapainēs tēs para ton mulon ("to the firstborn of the maidservant who is beside the mill"), using para ("beside") rather than "behind."

6. **"A great cry" (v. 6) — tse'aqah gedolah:** Hebrew vehayethah tse'aqah gedolah bekhol-erets Mitsrayim asher kamohu lo nihyathah vekamohu lo tosif. The noun tse'aqah ("cry, outcry, shriek") is the word used for cries of anguish and distress — the same root (ts-'-q) used for Israel's cry under oppression (2:23; 3:7, 9). The symmetry is devastating: Egypt cried out no such cry before and will never cry out such a cry again, just as Israel's cry of oppression was unique in its intensity. The oppressor's cry mirrors the oppressed's cry. The formula "such as has never been and such as shall never be again" (asher kamohu lo nihyathah vekamohu lo tosif) echoes the language of unprecedented uniqueness used for the locust plague (10:14) and the hail (9:18, 24) — each successive plague breaks the scale, but this final cry surpasses them all. LOGOS renders literally. The LXX renders estai kraugē megalē kath' holēn gēn Aiguptou ("there shall be a great cry throughout the whole land of Egypt").

7. **"Not a dog shall sharpen its tongue" (v. 7) — lo yecheratz-kelev leshono:** Hebrew uvekhol beney Yisra'el lo yecheratz-kelev leshono leme'ish ve'ad-behemah. This is a proverbial expression of absolute tranquility. The verb yecheratz (from charats, "to sharpen, to cut, to decide") in the Qal with kelev ("dog") as subject and leshono ("its tongue") as object produces the image of a dog sharpening or pointing its tongue — that is, barking, snarling, or growling. The proverb means: not even a dog will so much as growl in the direction of Israel. The contrast with Egypt's "great cry" (v. 6) is total: Egypt will shriek in unprecedented anguish while Israel will experience such perfect peace that not even a stray dog will disturb it. The purpose clause lema'an ted'un asher yafhleh YHWH bein Mitsrayim uvein Yisra'el ("so that you may know that the LORD makes a distinction between Egypt and between Israel") uses the verb yafhleh (Hiphil of palah, "to make distinct, to separate, to make wonderful"). This is the verb of divine discrimination — the same root used in 8:22 (vehifleti, "I will make distinct") and 9:4 (vehiflah, "the LORD will make a distinction"). The plague theology reaches its climax: every plague has discriminated between Egypt and Israel, and the final plague will demonstrate this distinction with maximum clarity — death on one side, undisturbed peace on the other. LOGOS renders "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue" to preserve the Hebrew idiom. The LXX renders ou grusei kuōn tē glōssē autou ("not a dog shall growl/mutter with its tongue"), using gruzō ("to growl, to grumble"), which captures the sense of hostile canine vocalization.

8. **"And he went out from Pharaoh in hot anger" (v. 8) — bechari-af:** Hebrew veyatse'u khol-avadekha elleh elai vehishtachavu-li lemor tse umol-ha'am asher-beraglekha ve'acharey-khen etse vayyetse me'im-Par'oh bechari-af. The verse presents a striking shift. Moses, still speaking to Pharaoh, describes a future scene: "All these servants of yours shall come down to me and bow down to me." The verb hishtachavu ("bow down, prostrate") is the deepest form of obeisance — Pharaoh's own courtiers will prostrate themselves before Moses. The phrase asher-beraglekha ("who are at your feet," i.e., "who follow you") identifies the departing people as those under Moses' authority. Moses conspicuously avoids saying that Pharaoh himself will bow — a diplomatic restraint that nonetheless implies Pharaoh's total capitulation. The narrator then records: vayyetse me'im-Par'oh bechari-af ("and he went out from Pharaoh in hot anger"). The phrase bechari-af (literally "in the burning of the nose/anger") describes intense, burning wrath. Whose anger? The grammar makes Moses the subject — Moses departs in rage. This is the only time in the plague narrative that Moses displays personal anger toward Pharaoh. The timing is significant: this departure fulfills 10:29 ("I shall not again see your face") and marks the final rupture of the Moses-Pharaoh relationship. LOGOS renders "in hot anger" for bechari-af. The LXX renders meta thumou ("with fury/wrath").

9. **"So that My wonders may be multiplied" (v. 9) — revot mofetay:** Hebrew vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh lo-yishma aleykhem Par'oh lema'an revot mofetay be'erets Mitsrayim. The infinitive revot (from ravah, "to be many, to multiply") with mofetay ("My wonders") articulates the divine purpose behind Pharaoh's refusal: the more Pharaoh resists, the more wonders God performs. Each refusal becomes an occasion for another demonstration of divine power. The noun mofet ("wonder, portent, sign") differs from ot ("sign"): mofet emphasizes the spectacular, awe-inspiring quality of the divine act, while ot emphasizes its communicative, sign-bearing function. Both terms have been used throughout the plague narrative (cf. 7:3, 9; 4:21), but mofet appears here in climactic position — the wonders are being multiplied to their fullest extent. This verse functions as a retrospective theological summary: the entire plague sequence, from blood to death-of-firstborn, has served the purpose of multiplying God's wonders in Egypt. LOGOS renders "so that My wonders may be multiplied." The LXX renders hopōs plēthunthōsin mou ta sēmeia kai ta terata ("so that My signs and wonders may be multiplied"), characteristically doubling "signs and wonders" where the MT has only "wonders."

10. **"And the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" (v. 10) — chazaq, Piel — FIFTH DIVINE HARDENING:** Hebrew vaychazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh velo-shillach et-beney Yisra'el me'artso. This is the fifth divine hardening (after 9:12; 10:1; 10:20; 10:27) and the fourth using chazaq Piel with YHWH as subject. The formula is identical to 10:20 and 10:27, but its placement here — at the close of the entire plague-announcement sequence — gives it climactic force. The addition of me'artso ("from his land") at the end is significant: the refusal is now territorial — Pharaoh refuses to release Israel from his land, asserting ownership over both the people and the territory. This is the last hardening formula before the Passover legislation of chapter 12. After this, the next mention of Pharaoh's heart will be in 14:4, 8 — at the Sea of Reeds — where the hardening will drive Pharaoh to pursue the departed Israelites to his own destruction. LOGOS renders with bold formatting to mark the divine agency. The LXX renders esklērunen de kurios tēn kardian Pharaō ("and the Lord hardened the heart of Pharaoh").

### Hardening Verb Tracking — Exodus 11

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | Subject/Agent | LOGOS Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **11:10** | **chazaq** | **Piel (causative)** | **YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart** | **"the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"** |

*Note: Exodus 11 contains a single hardening instance, positioned as the chapter's concluding verse. This placement is structurally significant: v. 10 serves as the capstone of the entire plague-announcement narrative from Exodus 7-11. It pairs with v. 9 to form a two-verse theological summary — God told Moses that Pharaoh would not listen (v. 9), and indeed the LORD strengthened Pharaoh's heart and he did not send out the sons of Israel (v. 10). The chapter thus closes the first major arc of the Exodus narrative (plague sequence) and opens the second (Passover and departure). The chazaq Piel with YHWH as subject has now appeared four times (9:12; 10:20; 10:27; 11:10), establishing the pattern that will recur at the Sea of Reeds (14:4, 8). The total divine hardening count now stands at five (adding 10:1, kaved Hiphil).*

### Cumulative Hardening Verb Table — Exodus 7–11

| Verse | Root | Form | Agent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7:3 | qashah | Hiphil | God (prospective) | "I will make hard" — announced, not yet enacted |
| 7:13 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after staff sign |
| 7:14 | kaved | Adjective | — (descriptive) | "is heavy" — God's diagnosis |
| 7:22 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after blood plague |
| 8:15 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after frogs removed |
| 8:19 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after gnats; magicians confess |
| 8:32 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after swarm removed |
| 9:7 | kaved | Qal (adj.) | Heart (descriptive) | "was heavy" — after livestock plague |
| **9:12** | **chazaq** | **Piel** | **GOD (active)** | **"the LORD strengthened" — FIRST DIVINE HARDENING** |
| 9:34 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after hail; labeled "sin" |
| 9:35 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — chapter closing |
| **10:1** | **kaved** | **Hiphil** | **GOD (active)** | **"I have made heavy" — SECOND DIVINE HARDENING; kaved Hiphil now divine; extended to servants** |
| **10:20** | **chazaq** | **Piel** | **GOD (active)** | **"the LORD strengthened" — THIRD DIVINE HARDENING; after locusts removed** |
| **10:27** | **chazaq** | **Piel** | **GOD (active)** | **"the LORD strengthened" — FOURTH DIVINE HARDENING; triggers final confrontation** |
| **11:10** | **chazaq** | **Piel** | **GOD (active)** | **"the LORD strengthened" — FIFTH DIVINE HARDENING; closes plague-announcement sequence** |

### Key Term Renderings — Exodus 11

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| nega echad | one more plague | v. 1; nega = "stroke, plague, touch"; echad = "one" — precisely one final blow |
| garesh yegaresh | he will surely drive you out | v. 1; Piel infinitive absolute + imperfect; emphatic expulsion |
| sha'al | ask | v. 2; "ask, request" — NOT "borrow"; no obligation of return |
| keley-khesef ukheley zahav | articles of silver and articles of gold | v. 2; keli = "article, vessel, implement" |
| chen | favor | v. 3; divinely bestowed grace/attractiveness |
| kachatsot hallaylah | about midnight | v. 4; from chatsah "to divide"; the dividing-point of the night |
| bekhor | firstborn | v. 5; the firstborn male, heir of the family |
| hashifchah | the maidservant | v. 5; female slave; lowest social station |
| harechayim | the millstones | v. 5; dual form; the hand-mill for grinding grain |
| tse'aqah gedolah | a great cry | v. 6; the cry of anguish; mirrors Israel's cry (2:23) |
| yecheratz | sharpen | v. 7; from charats; "sharpen the tongue" = bark/growl |
| yafhleh | makes a distinction | v. 7; Hiphil of palah; divine discrimination between Egypt and Israel |
| bechari-af | in hot anger | v. 8; lit. "in the burning of the nose"; Moses' wrath at departure |
| mofetay | My wonders | v. 9; spectacular divine portents; distinct from ot ("sign") |

---

### Theological Note: The Architecture of Exodus 11

Exodus 11 is the hinge of the entire Exodus narrative — the chapter that links the plague sequence (chs. 7-10) to the Passover-Exodus sequence (chs. 12-15). Its ten verses accomplish three major functions: the announcement of the final plague (vv. 1, 4-8), the preparation for departure (vv. 2-3), and the theological summation of the entire plague cycle (vv. 9-10).

The structural placement of chapter 11 within the larger narrative has long been debated. Verses 1-3 appear to be a divine communication to Moses that chronologically precedes the final confrontation of 10:24-29 — God tells Moses about the plague and the request for silver and gold before Moses delivers the announcement in 11:4-8. This has led many commentators to read 11:1-3 as a parenthetical flashback: during the conversation recorded in 10:24-29, Moses already possessed the knowledge communicated in 11:1-3, and it was this knowledge that gave him the confidence to declare "I shall not again see your face" (10:29). The speech of 11:4-8 then continues directly from 10:29 — Moses, before departing from Pharaoh's presence, delivers the final oracle of doom. The departure "in hot anger" (bechari-af, v. 8) then completes the scene begun in 10:24.

The plague announcement in vv. 4-8 is the only plague in the entire sequence that is both announced and executed by God directly. In all previous plagues, Moses or Aaron served as the human agent — stretching out a hand, striking with a staff, throwing soot into the air. For the death of the firstborn, God declares ani yotse ("I am going out") — the first-person active participle signaling personal, immediate divine action. No intermediary, no staff, no stretched-out hand. This underscores the uniqueness and finality of the tenth plague.

The social comprehensiveness of v. 5 — "from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits upon his throne to the firstborn of the maidservant who is behind the millstones" — demolishes the Egyptian social hierarchy. Death does not respect rank. The throne and the millstones are the two extremes of Egyptian society, and the plague traverses the entire spectrum. The inclusion of "every firstborn of the beast" extends the judgment into the non-human realm, reprising the livestock plague (9:1-7) but now with lethal specificity.

The contrast between Egypt's "great cry" (v. 6) and Israel's undisturbed peace (v. 7) is the most vivid expression of the distinction (haflayah) motif that has run through the entire plague narrative. The image of the dog that does not so much as sharpen its tongue is a masterpiece of understated comparison: Egypt screams in agony while Israel is so perfectly at peace that not even the most vigilant, most easily disturbed creature — the neighborhood dog — makes a sound.

The chapter's closing verses (9-10) form a chiastic summary of the entire plague cycle: God said Pharaoh would not listen (v. 9a), so that wonders would multiply (v. 9b); Moses and Aaron performed all these wonders (v. 10a), and the LORD strengthened Pharaoh's heart (v. 10b). The hardening and the wonders are presented as two sides of a single divine purpose — each hardening occasions another wonder, and each wonder deepens the revelation of who YHWH is. The plague sequence is now complete in its announced form. What follows is the Passover: the final act of judgment and liberation.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 11 (pages 51-52) presents several notable textual features. Most significantly, the LXX arrangement of chapter 11 differs from the MT. In the LXX tradition, portions of what the MT presents as 11:1-3 are embedded within the chapter 10 narrative — specifically, the instruction to ask for silver and gold articles appears earlier, integrated into the context of the darkness plague negotiations. This reflects an alternative editorial tradition that sought to resolve the chronological difficulty noted above (how Moses receives 11:1-3 during the conversation of 10:24-29). The Vaticanus text at v. 3 expands the description of Moses' greatness, reading kai ho anthrōpos Mōusēs megas egenēthē sphodra enantion tōn Aiguptiōn kai enantion Pharaō kai enantion tōn therapontōn autou ("and the man Moses became very great before the Egyptians and before Pharaoh and before his servants") — notably adding "before Pharaoh" (enantion Pharaō), which the MT lacks. This LXX expansion makes explicit what the MT leaves implicit: Moses' greatness is recognized even by Pharaoh himself, despite Pharaoh's hostility. In v. 5, the LXX reads heōs prōtotokou tēs therapainēs tēs para ton mulon ("to the firstborn of the maidservant beside the mill"), using the singular mulon ("mill") rather than the MT's dual rechayim ("millstones"). In v. 7, the LXX renders the dog proverb as ou grusei kuōn tē glōssē autou ("not a dog shall growl with its tongue"), using gruzō rather than a verb for "sharpening" — interpreting the idiom rather than rendering it literally. In v. 9, the LXX characteristically doubles the MT's single mofetay ("My wonders") into ta sēmeia mou kai ta terata ("My signs and wonders"), reflecting the common LXX pairing of sēmeion and teras as a hendiadys for divine portents. The Vaticanus manuscript shows the chapter 12 section beginning clearly, with a paragraph marker consistent with the MT's petuchah division.

--- LOGOS
